title: Dyeing with nettles for yellow-green
subtitle: Extracting green and yellow-green dye from fresh stinging nettle leaves and stems
excerpt: Stinging nettles produce a reliable yellow-green dye on alum-mordanted wool. The dye compound is in the leaves and upper stems. A nettle dye bath simmered at low temperature retains more of the green tone than a high-heat extraction, which pulls more yellow. Fresh nettles in spring and early summer give the strongest colour; dried nettles produce a paler, more yellow result.
sourceNotes: Nettle as a dye plant is documented in European botanical dyeing literature. Low-temperature green extraction technique is observation-led.

content.text: Nettle dye works best at a moderate simmer: high heat pulls yellow-green toward pure yellow, while a 70 to 80°C extraction holds more of the green. Alum-mordanted wool takes nettle dye well; unmordanted fibre picks up very little colour. The
is strained from the plant material before the fibre is added, preventing contact marks. A full
of the bath (pale liquor, good colour on fibre) indicates the dye has fully transferred.

items.name: Fresh stinging nettles (leaves and upper stems)
Long rubber or protective gloves (for handling fresh nettles)
Strainer or muslin cloth (for straining plant material)

content.text: Wear gloves when handling fresh nettles. Pack the leaves and stems loosely into the dye pot and cover with cold water. Bring slowly to 70 to 80°C and hold at that temperature for 45 minutes. Do not boil: a hard boil pulls more yellow and less green from the plant. The water will turn yellow-green.

Strain out all plant material through a strainer or muslin cloth into a clean pot. The remaining dye liquor should be clear yellow-green. Discard or compost the plant material. Return the dye liquor to the heat and maintain at 70 to 80°C.

Wet out the mordanted wool in warm water before adding to the dye pot. Submerge gently and hold at 70 to 80°C for 45 to 60 minutes, turning the fibre occasionally for even colour. Do not agitate vigorously: this felts wool. The colour on the fibre will look deeper when wet than when dry.

Remove the fibre and rinse in water of the same temperature as the dye bath to avoid felting. Rinse progressively cooler until the rinse water runs clear. Squeeze gently and dry flat out of direct sunlight. Nettle dye is moderately light-fast on alum-mordanted wool.

attrs.intro: Two common problems with nettle dyeing.

items.fix: Keep the temperature at or below 80°C throughout. A thermometer is essential for nettle green: the difference between 75°C and 95°C is the difference between green and yellow.
items.cause: Dye bath was too hot during extraction or dyeing.
items.symptom: Colour is yellow rather than green

items.fix: Use at least 200 g of fresh plant material per 100 g of fibre. Confirm that the fibre was mordanted with alum: unmordanted wool takes very little natural dye.
items.cause: Fibre was not mordanted, or too little plant material was used.
items.symptom: Very pale or no colour on the fibre

attrs.heading: Common problems
